data.title: Coterra Energy: Flexible Multi-Basin Oil And Gas Producer With Strong Free Cash Flow Generation
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Coterra Energy (CTRA) is an oil and gas producer with high-quality multi-basin assets across the US, with a stock that’s been relatively flat since their big deal with Cimarex Energy in late 2021, when they also renamed themselves Coterra.

Right now, CTRA seems to offer a good opportunity thanks to the attractive valuation, with natural gas and increased production thanks to recent deals leading to significant cash flows despite lower oil prices.

The company boasts their high-quality assets, with significant well cost reductions in all their basins since 2023 (21% for the company as a whole), low reinvestments, and an average inventory duration of ~15 years across their 3 basins. This multi-basin focus allows them to diversify their exposure and commodities, with the natural gas focus helping them withstand the oil fall recently and even leading to an increased guidance, while they can focus their investment efforts on whatever opportunity they find - wherever it is.

As they also show, this flexibility allows them to deliver resilient free cash flows in several environments, taking advantage of higher oil or gas prices when the other one falls. Despite this, we’ve also seen activist investor Kimmeridge saying in an open letter that Coterra’s board should appoint an independent, non-executive chair and refocus operations on its oil-rich Delaware Basin and avoid the “complexity, inefficiency, and valuation compression,” pointing out they trade below both Permian and gas-focused peers, as CTRA also shows.

The firm says Coterra should divest its Marcellus and Anadarko assets to become a pure-play Permian company, so basically the opposite of the current focus (as they actually boosted the Marcellus investment program by $7 million vs the February guidance).

Compared to peers, including three that I own and some very good ones, we see a very solid break-even, which is ultimately an advantage we see reflected even in their Q3 report, which came with very solid cash flows despite the falling oil prices.

Regarding the free cash flow, it came in at around $533 million (adjusted), expecting ~$2 billion in 2025 despite the significant ~$2.31 billion CAPEX expected in 2025, backed by solid natural gas prices and higher production expectations. This is assuming WTI at ~$60 and HH at ~$4.00, so it’s not some crazy expectation, and the previous guidance was assuming WTI at ~$71 and HH at $4.22, delivering $2.7 billion in FCF, so there’s definitely strong potential.

Financially, based on CTRA’s latest report, we see a good position, with little cash now but significant additions to their properties, with a manageable amount of debt. We see a significant increase in equity since Q4’24, with the acquisitions of certain assets from Franklin Mountain Energy Holdings LP and Avant Natural Resources LLC to boost their Permian assets, at a combined value of nearly $4 billion (~$1 billion in stock), expecting these to be more than 15% accretive to free cash flows per share in 2025 to 2027.

As we can see, their debt maturities are well-spread into the future, with quite low-cost debt and a history of conservative net leverage (currently at 0.8x vs 0.3x at the end of Q4’24). Still, we can see the longer-term debt coming at higher interest rates due to the current environment, which can also be an opportunity if they decide to refinance them again once interest rates are cut some more.

Dividends & Buybacks

Coterra pays a ~3.33% dividend yield, estimated to cost them ~$672 million in 2025, with modest buybacks that they’re restarting now in Q4 2025 and being “opportunistic in purchasing our shares” - with the extra FCF being planned for debt reduction and share repurchases. This year, they’re opting for $600 million in debt repayments over buybacks, while last year they spent ~$451 million for that instead, with minimal debt repayments.

Risks & Opportunities

Perhaps the most important opportunity for oil comes from OPEC and its allies recently agreeing to boost their oil production in December by the same amount as in October and November but deciding to pause its increases in Q1 2026, at least. These hikes were key in oil’s fall this year, as OPEC’s moves are ultimately one of the most important factors to keep in mind, so a potential reversal to the levels before these hikes has the potential of being a significant catalyst for the price of oil.

In my recent coverage of TotalEnergies (TTE), we’ve also seen a massive change in their annual energy outlook report, where they now estimate a new peak in oil demand that’s a whole decade further than last year’s expectations. There are several factors they name here, such as the slowdown in EV adoption following several moves, including lower incentives, Trump’s LNG permitting policies (also good for CTRA), and more, which is in line with the common trend we now see in oil giants, slowing down their investments in green ambitions and reverting to their reliable oil and gas operations.

Also, why I believe the pure-play focus on oil while selling their natural gas assets could be a mistake is because LNG still has massive potential, especially with a huge pipeline of projects becoming operational in the following years and Trump’s $750 billion US-EU LNG deal mentioned before. In such an environment, we could possibly even be looking at a revaluation of these assets just from this catalyst alone, especially if there’s a supply gap due to lower investments in production caused by lower oil prices today, as we’ve seen many oil companies do recently.

Meanwhile, another very important potential catalyst came from Trump’s sanctions on Russian oil, and TotalEnergies’ CEO even called them very important recently and even mentioned how the world is underestimating their impact, highlighting how premiums are rising as countries seek alternative shipment routes.

Again, these are the same sanctions that almost instantly pushed Chinese state-owned companies to halt their Russian oil imports and also made other Chinese oil buyers reduce their Russian oil purchases, which is a huge blow to Russia that can have significant effects on the future of their war in Ukraine, while they also forced Lukoil to sell its international assets shortly after these sanctions came, which is seen as the most consequential action on a Russian company since the war.

Continued US rate cuts can also be a significant catalyst to keep in mind, with a new one in October and some talks and expectations for a December cut. This can be great for oil stocks from several points of view, including boosting the demand for oil by increasing economic activity and decreasing their interest expenses ($206 million TTM) while also potentially fueling CTRA’s expansion through cheaper debt.

Still, these things take time, and right now we’ve been looking at the US Government going through what recently became its largest shutdown in history, causing the BLS to halt all data collection and publishing during it. As a result, the Fed has less up-to-date data during a time when I believe the impact of tariffs to the economy could’ve been clearer. And again, we’ve seen Powell citing some worries about inflation and unemployment back at the October meeting, so the timing was actually bad for this catalyst.

Another important factor to pay attention to, especially when we’re talking about an oil company, is Trump’s relationship with Venezuela, which can rapidly deteriorate once again (see 2018 Venezuelan elections and 2019 recognition of opposition leader Juan Guaidó as interim president), and even though Trump stated that he’s not planning any war with the country, he also said that Maduro’s days are numbered, so this will remain something that can change overnight.

Again, Venezuela is not just some random country with oil. The country is known to have the world's largest proven oil reserves - meaning roughly 4 times more than the US or Russia, or nearly 2 times the two combined! This comes with massive geopolitical importance, as Venezuela is one of the 5 founding members of OPEC, which would very likely not just sit and watch the US take over (with or without a war). Any issues here can lead to significant disruption in global oil trade (which can lead to higher prices), similar to or even more intense than wars in other regions relevant for oil, and I believe this can potentially be something of a magnitude we haven’t even seen before when it comes to oil prices.

So, although there are quite a few catalysts that can improve CTRA’s future, there are also near-term headwinds to keep in mind, especially related to the country’s economy and political uncertainty. Their management will have to navigate this environment carefully and should take a careful approach in balancing taking advantage of the potential opportunities without deteriorating their balance sheet at a time when the near-term future of rate cuts is under pressure, although I believe the long-term is still in their favor.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Devon Energy Corporation (DVN), APA Corporation (APA), Chord Energy Corporation (CHRD), Diamondback Energy (FANG), EQT Corporation (EQT), Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) and Permian Resources Corporation (PR) - many of which I already covered and rated positively (3 of which I actually own) - we see that they are not really the best in terms of most valuation metrics, with other peers coming at significantly more attractive P/CF or PB ratios, for example.

But still, we’re looking at relatively low ratios generally speaking, especially when comparing them to larger US peers.

Valuation

For CTRA’s valuation, starting by annualizing 2025’s FCF and getting to $1.8 billion (vs the $2 billion expected that adjusts the changes in assets and liabilities), let’s assume a 4.5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to the accretion of the recent acquisitions, rate cuts advancing and potential for other expansion projects, Big Beautiful Bill benefits, and support by natural gas and LNG’s potential, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.25% terminal growth rate. Assuming a WACC of 9% (estimated ~8.93% based on a cost of equity of ~10% and ~4.75% cost of debt), as well as $3.82 billion in net debt and 767 million shares outstanding. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $30.09 billion, an equity value of $26.27 billion, and an intrinsic value of $34.25 per share.

Conclusions

I rate CTRA a Buy thanks to their solid fundamentals, clear undervaluation, and great asset base with low-cost break-evens, although there are better peers in today’s market. There are risks associated with the macro environment and their industry, but CTRA remains a solid option to keep in mind for high-quality, relatively low-leverage oil and gas multi-basin exposure.

data.title: GEO Group: Contract Wins And Cash Flow Inflection Are Not Fully Priced In
data.description:
Recommendation

I recommend a Buy on The GEO Group, Inc. (GEO) with a 2-year target price of $30, which offers >40% IRR from current levels. The market is underestimating the 2026 potential earnings and free cash flow inflection, driven by record ICE contract wins, margin expansion and deleveraging. With leverage approaching <2.5x, I believe the path is clear for buybacks and dividend reinstatement. Even with my conservative assumptions, I see limited downside. Thus, I believe it is a Buy from here.

Q3 Earnings Summary

GEO's third quarter 2025 results are slightly mixed but still directionally positive. Diluted EPS hit $1.24, though this was inflated by a $232 million gain from asset divestitures. Revenue came in at $682mil, supported by steady occupancy and incremental contract wins which include the 1,000-bed Delaney Hall Facility in New Jersey and a joint-venture agreement to provide management services at the North Florida Detention Facility. Importantly, GEO has also paid down $275 million of its debt year to date through internal cash generation and bought back $41.6 million worth of stock. This has also prompted management to raise the repurchase authorization to $500 million which is bullish. Thus, this has reduced GEO's net leverage to ~3.2x.

Looking ahead, GEO has locked in a series of new and reactivated ICE contracts which totaled ~6,000 beds that is expected to contribute over $300 million annualized revenue. Management FY25 guidance suggest steady execution while FY26 looks more transformational.

Investment Thesis

I believe the market has misunderstood GEO's potential. The company is on the brink of significant earnings and cash flow inflection while the market has not fully priced that in. While the private prison industry is unloved, GEO is also evolving into a broader government services provider and benefit from structural shift like increased immigration enforcement.

For instance, record new contracts wins, acceleration in deleveraging and strategic capital returns of the business are drivers that would push the stock higher.

Record New Contracts

Founder George Zoley has highlighted that since 2025 the company added expanded or entered into new contracts that has expanded their annualized revenue by ~$460 million. These contracts include ICE processing centers reactivations, and also expansion of US Marshals capacity in new facilities such as Rivers Facility in NC and Big Spring: Cedar Hill in TX. By early 2026, they will be fully revamped and pushing GEO's revenue to $3 billion. In fact, these contracts are the largest amount of new business that the company has won in a single year!

Also, due to little analyst coverage, the market may anchor the company on its $2.5 billion run rate and past slow growth. Thus, when the company suddenly deliver ~18% revenue growth ($460 million/$2.5 billion), market is forced to accept that the company is not a stagnating company anymore. This also means that the operational leverage will be massive as these facilities were idle and thus margins would also tick-up higher.

Deleveraging Plans

GEO's aggressive debt payoff also provides a virtuous cycle. For instance, GEO has set the goal to bring its leverage below 3x and has cut its net debt by ~$275 million in 2025 YTD as shared by the founder during 3Q earnings. The company has two primary senior notes with an average interest of 9.4%. For illustration, every $100mil debt reduction, the company saves ~$9.4 million interest annually, which would flow to bottom line. Moreover, the market also has not appreciated once the leverage target is met, the Board authorization of $500 million buyback which is ~25% of outstanding shares would be a positive signal to the market.

The company also generates a lot of unlevered free cash flow. In fact, for the past few years, their annual unlevered FCF / Net income has been greater than 1x. Thus, I see this as a reliability signal that the company has the capability to deleverage and effectively enact its shareholder-friendly policies which would re-rate the shares further.

Operational Excellence and Margin Expansion

GEO also has been focusing on improving efficiency on their rehabilitation programs. Their Continuum of Care programs also have a track record of reducing recidivism which leads to saving government expenses and this led to increased performance incentives and contract extensions.

Another way to think about is that if GEO's facilities continue to outperform public benchmarks which is evident by ACA accreditation scores ~99%, GEO would have greater negotiating power for per diem increases and contract renewals. Moreover, the company has gone through a management reorganization in 2025 and support for the revenue growth from our new contract awards that would increase G&A expenses in the short term, but in the long run, as revenue expands, I anticipate operating leverage from higher occupancy and better cost control. This would also boost operating margins as GEO has been battling occupancy declines and is not able to cut cost quickly. But with the increased in occupancy, the tide has finally turned, and economies of scale would kick in.

Bull Case

In a bullish scenario, all of the drivers are aligned. GEO's earnings would compound at ~10%, reaching $2.0 EPS by 2027 which is very attainable from the new contracts and margin expansion. Leverage drops below ~2.5x which enabled a modest reinstatement of dividend. If GEO is also able to rebrand themselves to be more humanitarian, stigma may also ease, and this would also pull interest from ESG-oriented funds. I assigned a 15x P/E which is already at a wide discount from its current P/E level at 20x, I would get a target price of $30. This would mean imply a >40% IRR over two years.

Bear Case

My bear case centers on the policy and execution risk which may undermine my thesis. On the policy front, if a major adverse development from something like ICE deciding to significantly scale down detention due to judicial intervention would materially reduce the addressable market and cause the markets to react poorly. The ISAP electronic monitoring contract, which is currently a cash cow, may also be rebid in a way in 2026/27 that split among vendors that lowers margins.

GEO may also choose not to deleverage which would worsen the company's financial position. Assuming the company remains stagnant and never grows at all and cost savings did not materialize. its TTM 2027 EPS would remain constant at $1.73. I assigned a P/E of 10x which represent the market reacting poorly, which would give me a target price of $17.3, which still gives me an IRR of ~7%.

Thus, even if I assume a no-growth environment earnings growth with a significantly lower P/E multiple, the bull to bear scenario still has a positive asymmetrical result.

Valuation

GEO appears to be cheap relative to its sector peers. Most valuation multiples are trading at significant discounts to its sector median. For example, its forward P/E of 16.5x is ~19% lower than the sector median while EV/EBITDA (9.4x) and EV/EBIT (14.4x) trades at roughly ~30% and ~21% discount respectively. On a price to book basis, GEO is at 1.36x which is 55% below peers. However, its leverage ratios are not as fantastic as its debt to equity is at 107% and its net long debt to assets are at ~35% which are materially higher than its sector median.

However, part of my thesis is that the company would deleverage from here and that would reduce investors' concerns. Also, once leverage hits a certain level, shareholder-friendly policies would be introduced. Thus, I think at current multiples, buying at these levels is justified.

Risk

I believe the biggest risk is political as a single legislative act could severely curtail GEO's US business. For instance, ICE could be forced by law to phase out private contractors which was GEO's largest customer. Back in 2021, President Biden signed an executive order to reform the incarceration system and eliminate the use of private criminal detention centers which caused GEO to lose some contracts with DOJ. Thus, any form of contracts pull out would cause market sentiment to deteriorate further.

Conclusion

GEO is at a strategic inflection point with accelerating earnings and visible catalysts. While political risk remains a factor, the company seems to be trading at a cheaper valuation with real improving fundamentals, offering compelling upside. Management is also doing the right things by reducing its leverage and improving its financial position. Buy.

data.title: Animal Spirits Or Anxiety?
data.description:
By Anu Ganti

Big Tech and the strength of the Magnificent 7 stocks have powered the S&P 500 to 36 all-time closing highs through the end of October. Amid the euphoria, nervousness about the AI boom1 has sent jitters through the market, most recently with SoftBank’s sale of its stake in Nvidia.

The extreme level of mega-cap dominance is reflected through the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, whose trailing 12-month underperformance versus the S&P 500 plunged to 13% as of Oct. 31, 2025, with even worse levels seen in 1999 prior to the bursting of the tech bubble. With bubble fears in the air, it might be helpful to travel back to the past to understand the challenges of stock selection, especially throughout turbulent market regimes, with the knowledge that only a handful of stocks have outperformed over the long term.

Imagine it is Dec. 31, 1999. You are a professional portfolio manager and have witnessed five consecutive years of double-digit gains for the S&P 500, with a cumulative return of 253%. You are a bit apprehensive about whether these gains can continue and decide to seek five stock recommendations from your favorite Wall Street forecasters, denoted as Stocks 1-5,2 which you carefully consider.

Luckily for you, a genie appears to grant you one wish. You decide to ask which of the five stocks will be the best performer in the future. Unfortunately, our genie lacks expertise in one important respect: she knows a good bit about volatility but less about the returns of individual stocks.

The genie first reveals the future volatility of each stock, measured simply by the annualized standard deviation of daily returns. Exhibit 1 shows that all five stocks will be more volatile than the S&P 500, with standard deviations ranging from 59% (Stock 1) to 26% (Stock 5).

You’re a bit nervous about the high volatility of the first stock, but Stock 5, while still more volatile than the S&P 500, looks relatively more benign. The genie next tells you about the frequency of large losses in Exhibit 2, which shows the probability of the stocks declining by at least 1% or at least 5% on a single day. Stock 1 is the most likely of the group to have large daily losses, which has increased your anxiety.

It is now Nov. 13, 2025, and it’s time to evaluate how well each of the forecasters did and how well you would have done to have taken their advice. Exhibit 3 tells us the actual identity of the five stocks and their total return since Dec. 31, 1999.

Stock 1, Nvidia (NVDA), was the best performer in our study,3 with a stunning cumulative performance of more than 200,000%, followed by Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT). Stock 5, Exxon (XOM), although the least volatile, underperformed the S&P 500.4

On a risk dimension, the best performer was the most volatile holding. Volatility tests an investor’s conviction, and sometimes the stocks you will have most wanted to own are the hardest to hold.

This psychological difficulty is exacerbated for professional asset managers, who, acting as fiduciaries for their clients, may face challenges convincing them to stay confident when the market moves against their favor.

Going back to Dec. 31, 1999, you would not have known that tough times were soon in order, with the S&P 500 about to post declines for the next three consecutive years, followed later by the Global Financial Crisis of 2008.

Much later would be the COVID-19-related downturn in 2020, the losses in 2022, and most recently, the tariff-related tumult in early April 2025. Over long horizons, most stocks underperformed the market,5 and we know that most active managers underperformed with them, perhaps because historically, holding onto volatile stocks through painful periods of underperformance has required courage when one’s natural instinct is to sell.

1 The Astonishing Bull Market Will End One Day. Are You Ready? - NY Times

2 The stocks recommended can, but do not have to be a member of the S&P 500 at the time of this exercise.

3 The prescience of the forecaster who recommended Stock 1 is admirable, but we know that stock market forecasting is notoriously difficult, as any reader of our SPIVA® Scorecards will recognize.

4 Nvidia and Amazon joined the S&P 500 in November 2001 and November 2005, respectively.

5 Bessembinder, Hendrik. “Which U.S. Stocks Generated the Highest Long-Term Returns,” Nov. 11, 2024.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CAE: Civil Aviation Softness Masks A Defense Upswing And A Deep Value Entry
data.description:
CAE (CAE, CAE:CA), the global leader in flight training and simulation, has bright growth prospects on mid-to-long-term demand drivers in the commercial aviation and defense industries. However, since my last report, the stock has remained flat while the S&P 500 rose 6.1%. I believe the underperformance relative to the company’s execution and growth prospects does provide an attractive entry point. However, given that the company does not quite perform in line with its prospects, keeping a close eye on earnings and industry dynamics is important. In this report, I discuss the Q2 2026 earnings, the growth drivers for the company, and an update to my price target.

Dollar figures in this report are Canadian dollars, unless mentioned otherwise.

CAE Q2 Earnings Hindered By Civil Aviation Softness

CAE’s second quarter sales rose 9% to $1.24 billion, while gross margins expanded modestly by 20 basis points to 25.8%. Operating income surged 31% to $155.3 million. Excluding $30.9 million in restructuring charges in the comparable period last year, operating income grew 4%, indicating margins falling from 13.1% to 12.6%, driven by higher selling, general, and administrative expenses.

Civil Aviation revenues rose 5% to $670 million, while adjusted operating income declined 6%. The increase in sales was driven by the results of SIMCOM being added to the consolidated results as CAE increased its ownership last year and higher second-hand simulator sales. Utilization of flight simulators dropped from 70% to 64%, while the training capacity and thus fixed cost rose. Furthermore, there were six fewer full-motion flight simulator deliveries to customers.

Gross profit grew 5% to $198.6 million, indicating stability in the margins. Adjusted income declined, driven by lower demand for business and commercial services, while depreciation and amortization have increased in combination with higher SG&A.

For Civil Aviation, the long story short is that the company had lower margin growth driven by second-hand flight simulator sales, and demand for training solutions is currently soft. The company ended the quarter with an $8.5 billion backlog compared to $6.7 billion a year ago.

In the defense segment, CAE has restructured its business and started focusing on bigger and higher-margin defense contracts. Previously the company had legacy contracts in the pipeline that were not profitable. Those contracts are not less impactful, as some have been completed, and others have been derisked, while new backlog flowing into the revenues comes with a higher margin. Revenues rose 8% to $76.7 million, driven by higher activity on existing programs and ramp up of new programs in North America. Gross profit increased 19% to $18.9 million, driven by the better contract mix. Adjusted operating income grew 41% to $13.5 million on better operating leverage.

Market Dynamics Unchanged: Civil Aviation Softness, Defense Transition

The market dynamics remain unchanged compared to my assessment in the prior quarter. Civil Aviation training demand remains soft with a book-to-bill ratio of 0.88x and 1.08x when considering backlog adjustments. So, it is definitely a soft spot, but in the long term, demand is firmly supported by a growing global airplane fleet as well as training required for new pilots.

The defense business has been pressured by legacy contracts for quite some time. These contracts were fixed-price contracts agreed on before the pandemic, and after the pandemic, cost estimates rose partially also due to inflation, and that pressured the defense business. Currently, three legacy contracts remain to be completed, but we are already seeing better backlog flowing through the system, resulting in a substantial improvement in sales and margins. The adjusted backlog is $11.6 billion compared to $11.4 billion a year ago. The order intake was $555.8 million, bringing the book-to-bill ratio to roughly 1x.

CAE Stock Remains A "Buy"

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Figures below are in USD.

For CAE, I have a $32.30 target in the base case and $37.36 in the more bullish scenario. EBITDA estimates for 2026-2028 declined by 1.8% with a 2.4% downward revision this year, 1% next year, and 0.4% in the year after. CARG has remained more or less stable at 7.8%. For free cash flow, estimates have decreased by 0.5%, with a 3% decline this year, 0.3% next year, but an upward revision of 1.1% by FY28. CAGR has improved modestly from 8.9% to 9.3%. So, I am maintaining my "Buy" rating. My prior base case and more bullish targets were $33.37 and $37.93, so there is a downward revision on the price targets, but at 18%-36%, the upside remains compelling.

The key metrics show that EBITDA is expected to rise in the years ahead, but on stable margins, the increase from FY25 to FY26 is expected to be modest. The same holds for free cash flow, with a weaker free cash flow conversion expected this year, but this is expected to grow in the years ahead as CapEx as a percentage of operating cash flow will come down. We note that the company should be able to bring leverage down substantially, showing a strong balance sheet.

Conclusion: CAE Remains Attractive For The Long-Term

CAE’s results do show softness in civil aviation and robust growth in defense. That is seemingly something that is expected throughout the year, with little margin growth expected this year. However, the investment case remains compelling, as CAE is undervalued against aerospace and defense peers, and demand for its products and services is supported by longer-term trends.

data.title: Universal Technical Institute: Concorde Deregulation Signals New Upside
data.description:
The market still treats Universal Technical Institute (UTI) as a traditional vocational school specializing in mechanics and diesel, ignoring the transformation it has made through its acquisitions, such as Concorde Career Colleges, which represents a pivotal point in our thesis. During the Q3 call, the company revealed that it had satisfied the DOE requirements for lifting the growth restrictions imposed on Concorde. Therefore, since the market had lowered its growth expectations for this segment because of those regulatory restrictions, it should reassess the stock's valuation after lifting them.

Concorde is an educational institution that focuses on training specialists in the healthcare sector, which grows by 20% compared to 12% in the traditional segment. With the recent price decline due to profit-taking, the stock represents an excellent entry point.

The Shift To a Platform

Jerome Grant, the CEO of UTI, launched a plan known as North Star, which stands on three fundamentals: Growth, Diversification, and Optimization, with a special focus on diversification to reduce reliance on one segment (Auto & Diesel). The company executed a series of acquisitions in several fields, the first was in 2021 when it acquired MIAT College of Technology, which specializes in Airframes & Powerplant for $26 million. Then, in 2022, it was a turning point when it acquired Concorde Career Colleges for $50 million. Concorde operates in the healthcare sector, including nursing and dentistry etc. This deal added revenues that reached $180 million at that time. Importantly, Grant's vision indicates the goal is to integrate these entities with their systems into each other to build a unified platform that addresses the critical shortage of skilled labor, which will reduce costs as spending will be concentrated on a unified entity with higher efficiency instead of three separate ones. Consequently, increasing profit margins over time.

The Regulatory Environment

The vocational education sector has been living under a strict Gainful Employment rule. The goal of it is to ensure that educational programs do not burden graduates with debts they can't pay, so it stipulates that annual loan installments for graduates must be less than 8% of their total annual income, and also demands that graduate income must exceed the average income of a high school graduate in the same state.

Consequently, some believe that these rules will reduce the number of new students due to their inability to meet the requirements for federal loans. However, UTI programs are different as the school is originally designed to solve the severe labor shortage in fields such as mechanics, aviation, and nursing, where they offer well-paying jobs that can easily pass GE requirements. The other matter is that when the company acquired Concorde in 2022, it had restrictions subject to the Provisional Program Participation Agreement (PPA) that prevented it from expanding into new programs. However, in Q3, the management mentioned that DOE lifted these restrictions, which limited its growth. As a result, it can now expand into new programs and branches, accelerating its growth by much more than 20%.

Competition

UTI owns a competitive advantage represented in its strong relationships with major manufacturers. It has specialized training partnerships and programs with companies like Ford Motor Company (F), Bayerische Motoren Werke Aktiengesellschaft (OTC:BMWKY), and Toyota Motor Corporation (TM). These partnerships ensure that its curriculum is continuously updated. It also provides a factory-certified certificate to the graduate, giving them priority in employment with these companies, while its competitors, like Lincoln Educational Services Corporation (LINC), do not own this depth of exclusive partnerships.

In addition, the Concorde acquisition is considered a new moat for the company in the healthcare sector. While LINC tries to build nursing programs (LPN) from scratch, Concorde is a well-known brand that includes 17 campuses and 7,500 students; thus, UTI is years ahead of its competitors in this area. Furthermore, the acquisition of MIAT College of Technology allowed the company to apply in specialized programs that community colleges cannot easily access due to strict regulatory approvals, such as Airframe and Powerplant, which requires FAA approval.

Financials

UTI has a strong record exceeding analysts' expectations. In Q3, UTI's traditional segment revenues reached $131.5 million while Concorde segment generated $72.8 million for a total of $204.3 million, noting that Concorde, which represents 36% of the company's revenues, grew by 20.7% vs 12.2% for UTI's traditional segment. This means that it grows at a faster pace, indicating greater demand for healthcare programs. As for adjusted EBITDA, it reached $25.3 million with an increase of 37.3% YoY, meaning that every new dollar from revenues generates a higher profit than before. Every quarter, the revenue mix shifts more towards Concorde, which is faster and has higher profitability than the traditional segment, contributing to increasing the total growth rate and profitability for the company. In general, UTI is transforming into a fully vocational education company with a leadership in the healthcare segment.

Guidance

The company expects the strong performance to continue during Q4, driven by Concorde after the DOE lifted the regulatory restrictions and continued improvement in operational performance in the traditional segment. Regarding the full year, the management expects revenues between $830 and $835 million, representing +15% growth. It also expects +$124 million for the adjusted EBITDA. The removal of restrictions on Concorde will enable it to accelerate geographic expansion and launch new programs in 2026, making revenues lean more towards the high-demand/growth healthcare segment, which will translate into higher revenues and profits during the coming period.

Valuation

According to the company's guidance, it expects adjusted EBITDA of $124 million for 2025. UTI has an EV/EBITDA of approximately 12x. However, the multiple of its closest competitor, LINC, is 20x. I will assume the average of 16x to put a margin of safety. With that in mind and after adding net cash of $40 million, the equity value comes to $2024 million, or $37 per share (shares outstanding at 54.4 million).

The price target represents a potential increase of more than 20% from the current trading levels $30.

Risks

No investment is without risk, so the first risk relates to excessive optimism regarding the new regulations for Gainful Employment. The regulations may be more stringent than anticipated, and the company may fail to pass some of its graduate income tests. New students may find it difficult to obtain federal loans, which will inevitably impact revenue. However, the high demand from companies for UTI graduates means there is a severe labor shortage, and therefore, the salaries offered will be sufficient to pass the tests.

After acquiring two large companies (Concorde and MIAT), if UTI fails to integrate their systems into a single platform, the company will have to manage each company's system separately. This will increase operating expenses instead of reducing them. Furthermore, a failed integration could lead to operational conflicts and delays in decision-making, which will damage the brand's reputation and slow down the enrollment of new students.

Some bears believe the company is experiencing a slowdown in growth, citing a 2.8% increase in new student enrollment in the third quarter. However, a closer look reveals that it's the traditional UTI segment that has slowed growth, while the more important revenue-generating metric, average active student growth, has been 12.7%, reaching 18.8% for the Concorde segment.

Conclusion

UTI is no longer just a mechanical school; it's on its way to becoming a diverse workforce platform, driven by Concorde, after it gets released from restrictions. In my opinion, the recent pullback after the stock's significant surge is due to profit-taking, making it a buying opportunity for long-term investors. However, investors should watch any management comments on Concorde's expansion plan following the deregulation, as well as monitor the number of new students, which is expected to accelerate. Also, be sure to follow any updates from the company or DOE regarding UTI's debt-to-Earnings tests (Gainful Employment), which is expected to be positive.

data.title: BRT Apartments Corp: Cheap With Caveats
data.description:
Shares of multi-family REIT BRT Apartments Corp. (BRT) have lost roughly 45% of their value since August 2022 as impending higher refinancing rates and oversupply in the Sun Belt markets have weighed. Those undercurrents have slowed a management team that had been actively refurbishing distressed properties, selling them for a profit, and employing the funds to buy out its partners.

With its first meaningful mortgage rollovers beginning in 2026, two recent acquisitions, a 6.9% yield with a payout ratio of 67%, and the recent and continual insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis/recommendation follows below.

Company Overview:

BRT Apartments Corp. is a Great Neck, New York-based real estate investment trust (REIT) with the bulk of its revenue generated from its ownership and operation of 21 Class B or better multi-family complexes located primarily in the Sun Belt. As of the end of the third quarter, it owned or held interests in 31 properties encompassing 8,311 units across 11 states, including two 80% interests purchased in 2H25. BRT was formed in 1972 and went public in 1973 with its first trade executed at $22.88 per share, after giving effect to a 1-for-3 reverse stock split in 1986. Shares of BRT trade around $14.50 a share, equating to an approximate market cap of $290 million.

Owing to its approach of purchasing older, distressed assets in areas (like the Sun Belt) with strong demographics and investing capital to refurbish them, the REIT’s 5,420 wholly owned units are not particularly young, with the properties averaging over 24 years of age with a carrying value of $605.3 million at the end of Q2 '25. The average monthly rent on the wholly owned portfolio in H1 '25 was $1,365, representing an increase of 1.2% versus $1,349 in H1 '24. Over the same period, occupancy improved from 93.7% to 93.9%. Between those units and its partial interest in 2,527 (1,249 net) units – not counting its two H2 '25 acquisitions – BRT generated rental revenue of $57.4 million in H1 '25, up 0.2% from H1 '24. Putting it altogether, the REIT accounted for H1 '25 adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) of $0.75 per share versus $0.70 a share in the prior year period, while same store net operating income (NOI) fell 0.6% to $30.7 million. In short, there wasn’t much to report in 1H25. This continued in the third quarters, whose results were recently posted.

Recent Approach And Share Price Performance:

BRT’s portfolio, being comprised primarily of wholly owned properties, is a relatively recent approach, as it embarked on a strategy of buying out its partners in 2021. As a reference, at YE20, BRT possessed only eight apartments outright and held 32% to 90% interests in 31 other multi-family facilities.

Leveraging the historically low-interest rate environment, its renovations of older, distressed apartments allowed BRT to easily flip some of its joint venture properties for a significant profit and use those proceeds to buyout partners in other properties. Its scheme was further aided by a significant demographic shift out of urban centers on the coasts to the Southeast and Texas. As such, the market applauded the REIT’s pursuit of more consolidated ownership and its advantaged geography, driving shares of BRT from $6.00 during the throes of the pandemic-induced selloff in March 2020 to a 15-year high of $25.67 in August 2022.

However, that approach was halted after mortgage (and all interest) rates surged off historically low levels throughout 2022, as REIT investors demanded higher yields when the industry’s borrowing costs rose. And in many extremely hot markets like the Sun Belt, capital rushed in to fill the void, creating an abundance of supply in certain markets, limiting landlords’ capacity to raise rents, pushing cap rates below borrowing costs. In fact, given the lag in construction from start to completion, 592,000 units were completed in the U.S. during 2024, marking the highest level in 50 years. With higher financing rates and oversupply lowering its occupancy rate from over 96% to below 94%, shares of BRT fell 45% from their 2022 high, bottoming at $14.17 in August 2025, representing a 54-month low. They haven’t rebounded much at all, currently off 45% from that peak.

That said, in some instances, the market dynamics are starting to take a turn to the positive. In response to the recent saturation, 2025 U.S. apartment starts are projected to be down nearly 50% from their 2022 top of 531,000, with declines in the Sun Belt region expected to be meaningfully above the national average. Furthermore, the rent-versus-buy proposition in America is heavily weighted towards renting, with markets in the Sun Belt, such as Charlotte, Houston, Dallas/Ft. Worth, Raleigh/Durham, Atlanta, and Nashville all representing 50% to 60% discounts to rent. So with the continuing headwind (for now) of record supply meeting the tailwind of extreme cost to own, BRT is trading at just under 10 times TTM Adj. AFFO of $1.48 per share.

Against those general market dynamics, BRT has 21% of its mortgages outstanding (totaling $108.9 million) at a weighted average interest rate of 4.27% that will rollover between July 1, 2025, and December 31, 2026. Its July 2025 80% acquisition of a 214-unit multi-family facility in Auburn, Alabama for $36.5 million (including the assumption of an interest-only $24 million mortgage) provides some insight into its future refinancing outlook, with the interest rate on the fixed rate loan that matures in 2032 at 5.38%, somewhat in line with the 5.22% interest-only rate it received as part of a $27.4 million mortgage financing executed for a property in Georgia during 2024. That mortgage matures in 2031. With a weighted average interest rate of 4.26% on its entire portfolio and a weighted average term to maturity of 3.4 years (as of June 30, 2025), this undercurrent of higher refinancing rates will continue to erode BRT’s future FFO and AFFO calculations by $0.03 to $0.06 per annum, ceteris paribus.

Also creating a bit of a headwind is the fact that the REIT’s management team has not engaged the Street since early 2024 and does a subpar job of keeping the investor relations section of its website current, making it challenging to obtain insight into its current thinking. BRT is a family affair, with former Chairman of the Board and current director Fredric Gould’s two sons Jeff and Matt in the positions of CEO and senior vice president, respectively. Their cousin, Mitchell, is an executive vice president. Through their Gould Investors L.P., personal stakes, and other investment entities, they own a ~27% stake in the REIT.

Q2 '25 Financials:

BRT posted Q2 '25 FFO of $0.29 per share and AFFO of $0.36 per share on revenue of $24.2 million versus FFO of $0.29 per share and AFFO of $0.35 per share on revenue of $23.9 million in Q2 '24. As for the quarter, there was very little to report, with both property adds occurring after the close of the quarter.

Q3 '25 Financials:

BRT Apartments posted its Q3 results on November 8th. FFO fell from the second quarter to just 28 cents a share of FFO. Revenues were flat on a year-over-year basis to $24.43 million. There were very few other data points provided with the company's third quarter press release.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

As for BRT’s balance sheet, the carrying value on its apartment investments of $602.1 million and cash of $23.6 million were offset by mortgages and related obligations of just under $500 million, as of the close of Q3. After the announcement of its latest purchase, an 80% ownership interest in a 150-unit complex in Savannah, Georgia, BRT had $15 million outstanding on a revolving credit facility, which it plans to pay off through a refinancing in December 2025.

BRT’s two quarterly disbursements of $0.25 per share in 1H25 were easily covered by its AFFO of $0.75 per share, representing a current yield of 6.9% and a solid payout ratio of 67%.

BRT has a small Wall Street following. Colliers Securities reiterated its Hold rating and $16 price target on the stock following Q3 results hitting the wires. B Riley Financial reiterated its Buy rating on the stock in late September but lowered its price target from $21.00 a share to $19.50. In early April, Citizens JMP reissued its Buy rating and $24 a share price target on BRT. On average, sell-side analysts expect BRT to generate FFO of $1.17 a share on revenue of $95.8 million in FY25, followed by FFO of $1.21 a share on revenue of $99.7 million in FY26.

The Gould clan is perpetually bullish on its stock, through additions to Gould Investors, L.P. and personal accounts. This entity has purchased approximately $1.5 million in shares so far in the fourth quarter.

Verdict:

For an investor looking for yield, BRT is a decent play, returning 6.7% with a currently solid 67% payout ratio. That said, AFFO per share will erode going forward as it is compelled to gradually refinance its low-interest mortgages, inflating its payout ratio. For those betting on Fed rate reductions coming to the rescue, there is no guarantee that the reduction of an overnight rate will drive rates lower at the back-end of the yield curve. In fact, even with the 10-year treasury’s (US10Y) recent move nearer 4%, its yield is roughly flat since the Fed began its easing cycle in the summer of 2024, a period that has seen the Fed Funds rate decline by 125 basis points to 4.25%.

That said, although its yield is solid and its forward P/E on FY26E FFO of 12 is investible, the price appreciation outlook for its shares seems muted given the aforementioned headwinds – namely, oversupply leading to pressure on rents and occupancy, as well as higher refinancing rates. With very low volume in its stock, option activity is nearly non-existent, making a covered call basically non-viable. As such, due to muted price appreciation potential, no investment is recommended.

data.title: Palm Harbour Capital Q3 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear fellow investors,1

During the third quarter, the fund gained 1.57% (30/09/2025). We do not have a stated benchmark in our Key Investor Information Document (KIID) and therefore cannot comment on relative performance. We leave it up to you to decide. We note the above number appears lower than European and global benchmarks. Our last reported NAV at quarter end was 18.76 (30/09/2025), +1.57% from the closest reported NAV at the second quarter end of 18.47 (30/06/2025). This brings our year-to-date NAV return to +10.94% (27/12/2024 to 30/09/2025). Inception to quarter end NAV return was 87.6% or 10.2% compounded annual return. We are extremely optimistic about our portfolio’s prospects and believe we will reach our compound return aspiration over time. Our fund’s composition is unlike any index, and we are unlikely to perform in a similar manner.

The third quarter was similar to prior years with the summer mixed but positive (our fund hit its high of 19.09 on the 22nd of August) whilst September and October showed slight underperformance. Investor attention remains heavily concentrated on artificial intelligence and a narrow group of related equities. This has sidelined much of the broader market, including many of the cash - generative businesses we own. Traditional macro themes such as interest rates, monetary policy, and even crypto have faded from prominence, underscoring the dominance of the AI narrative.

An unusual feature of the quarter was the parallel rally in both equities and gold — two asset classes that historically exhibit little correlation. Explanations range from concerns over fiscal deficits and de-dollarisation to inflationary pressures and political gridlock. In our view, the most plausible driver is excess liquidity: governments, particularly the US, continue to run large deficits, while interest rates remain below nominal GDP growth. This backdrop sustains elevated inflationary risks and supports gold prices.

Recent commentary has started to ask whether we are in a bubble, or at least an AI bubble. We take no view on this, but we do think valuations, particularly in the US and India, are at nosebleed levels.

Some argue that valuations are less relevant today, or that historical comparisons no longer apply. The prevailing view is that “this time is different”: companies are stronger, balance sheets carry less leverage, and profitability is robust. While we agree that today’s environment differs from the late-1990s technology bubble, we see certain parallels. Beyond elevated valuations, the concentration of capital in artificial-intelligence-related stocks — primarily within the “Magnificent Seven” is even greater than the technology sector’s concentration in 2000.

Bulls contend that these are not profitless speculative ventures. Yet questions remain. OpenAI, for example, is loss-making and its revenue model remains unproven, even as it strikes multi-billion-dollar deals without clear funding sources. Nvidia (NVDA), though highly profitable, has engaged in what some analysts describe as “circular” transactions to2 sustain growth.

At the center of this dynamic are the big four AI data center power users Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT). Two observations stand out. First, while these businesses are highly profitable, over half of their cash flows are dependent on advertising. In past downturns, advertisers cut spending in traditional media first, sparing digital platforms. Today, however, the advertising pie is almost entirely in the hands of the digital platforms. In the next downturn, they may not be insulated; a broad pullback in advertising could directly impact their ability to fund AI investment.

Second, the capital-light nature that once defined these companies is changing. The largest four are projected to spend roughly $370 billion on AI capital expenditure in 2025 - roughly 70% of their last twelve month combined cash flow from operations - transforming them into more capital-intensive businesses. History suggests that industries undergoing rapid technological build-outs — railroads, electrification, internet infrastructure — often overinvest, leading to poor returns. Whether current spending proves excessive or insufficient remains uncertain, but the risks are clear.

We remain cautious in these debates, preferring to monitor developments rather than chase momentum. Meanwhile, opportunities abound in overlooked small and mid-sized companies with strong cash flows and attractive valuations. Our portfolio is deliberately positioned in such businesses, particularly across Asia, Latin America, and select European cyclical sectors. We are confident these holdings offer compelling long-term prospects and meaningful diversification away from US mega-cap exposure.

During the quarter we exited our position in Ocean Wilsons, following the family’s decision to merge the company with its investment trust rather than return cash to shareholders. We also sold Syensqo, the Solvay specialty chemical spin-off, reallocating capital to cyclical exposures with stronger cash-flow prospects. In addition, we exited The Italian Sea Group due to a slowdown in new orders. On the buy side, we initiated positions in Cirsa (Spanish IPO), Converge ICT (Philippines), Indofood (Indonesia), and Vivendi.

The quarter was relatively quiet with few major events in the portfolio. One exception was Norma, the German clamp and connector company, who announced the sale of their water business for $1 billion dollars, which will derisk the balance sheet, allow for a large shareholder return, and buy the company time to turnaround the ailing auto supplier part of their business.

At quarter-end our portfolio had more than 91% upside to our estimated NAV and was trading at a weighted average P/E of 9.1x, FCF/EV yield of 16% and a return on tangible capital of 24%.
Contributors Detractors
Esprinet SpA 100 bps Loma Negra -73 bps
Brightstar Lottery 92 bps RHI Magnesita -39 bps
Atalaya Mining 73 bps The Italian Sea Group -38 bps
Danieli & C Savers 58 bps Compagnie De l'Odet -35 bps
Caltagirone 55 bps Ginebra San Miguel -30 bps

The top contributor during the quarter was Esprinet (+46.3%, +100 bps), the Italian electronics distributor, introduced in our fourth quarter 2019 letter. Esprinet reported sales growth of 4.9% and a sequential gross profit margin improvement of 18 bps compared to the first quarter 2025 and 25 bps compared to second quarter 2024. Some cost increases related to the carry over effect from salary raises and own-brand marketing campaigns reduced EBITDA margin to 4.35% from 4.47% the previous year. Management set guidance during the “liberation day” panic and likely low-balled to be safe. Based on the first two quarters, guidance looks obtainable, and management commented positively on July and August trading, with mid-single digit growth in Italy and double-digit growth in the Iberia segment, which could signal a further market recovery. There is hope that the windows 10 run-off has re-ignited the PC renewal cycle. Management sounded more optimistic about the second half, and we welcomed the positive news after a couple of sluggish quarters.

The second largest contributor was Brightstar Lottery (IGT) (+32.8%, +92 bps), the Italian American lottery provider, which we introduced in our first quarter 2020 letter and updated in our first quarter 2024 letter. During the quarter, Brightstar reported several meaningful contract wins and extensions, following the nine-year Lotto operator license in Italy, including an eight-year contract in Missouri, and several multi-year instant ticket printing contract extensions. The $4 billion sale of the Gaming and Digital segment to Apollo (APO) was finalised during the quarter, and the company paid the $3 per share special dividend. Moreover, management confirmed guidance and launched a $250 million accelerated share repurchase program. We believe that investors low expectations are already reflected in the share price, and we continue to see a compelling risk-reward opportunity supported by organic growth, jackpot normalisation, rest of world expansion and c. 5% (tax-free) dividend yield.

The third significant contributor was Atalaya Mining (+36.9% +73 bps), the Spanish pure play copper producer. Sales increased 34.6% year-on-year supported by higher copper concentrate volumes sold and lower offsite costs, despite lower realized prices and a stronger EUR relative to USD. Operating leverage helped the business which delivered a 108% EBITDA increase. Management slightly upgraded production and reduced cash3 cost guidance. During the second quarter, waste stripping activities continued at San4 Dionisio with total material mined of one million tonnes (initial mining following the environmental authorisation in May 2025). Production is expected to accelerate in the second half. It is worth noting that San Dionisio represent a key asset for Atalaya to increase copper production by sourcing higher-grade material (0.78% Copper Resource grade versus 0.37% of its main producing asset), which has the potential to deliver both higher volumes and increase margins. We remain positive on the long-term prospects5 of copper due to supply restriction (historical under investment, licencing complexities) and booming demand (energy transition, technology, Artificial Intelligence) and we like Atalaya’s optionality from the promising pipeline of copper projects.

The fourth largest contributor was Danieli (+20% +58 bps), the Italian steel plant maker and steel producer, introduced in our third quarter 2020 letter. Plant Making reported in line with initial guidance, but ahead consensus with both sales and EBITDA increasing by 2.7% and 21.7% respectively, despite headwinds from extraordinary expenses related to customer disputes and the forced closure of the Russian subsidiary. Steel Making reported 28.9% EBITDA decline, which was lower than management’s expectations mainly due to headwinds related to competitively high energy cost in Italy (mostly in second half 2024). Measures taken by the Italian government to improve industrial energy prices aided profitability during the year. This helps underpin a more positive outlook for next year, as does the potential for Chinese steel tariffs. Danieli’s order book remains robust, even before factoring in several major contracts that have been announced but are still awaiting financing. This includes a €350 million intra-group order from ABSS. p. A., which is intended to increase installed EAF steel production capacity and strengthen overall competitiveness. The EU commission finally has a plan to protect the EU steel industry from unfair competition, targeting Chinese imports which have hurt the industry for quite some time. If the proposal is implemented, the EU steel industry is expected to see much better days - supporting both of Danieli’s segments. Excluding the net cash position (24% of market capitalization), Danieli continues to trade on a double-digit FCF yield which we consider attractive.

The fifth largest contributor was Caltagirone (+18.4%, +55 bps), the Italian holding company with its main asset the listed white cement company Cementir, which was introduced in our first quarter 2021 letter. As a reminder, 56% of Caltagirone’s NAV is related to Cementir (at quarter end market prices), the Italian cement producer, and 24% to Generali, the Italian insurer. The share prices of both underlying assets hit all-time high prices during the quarter hence Caltagirone share price followed suit. Despite the multiyear rally, Caltagirone continues to trade at 50% discount to the quoted sum-of-parts and an even deeper discount to our estimated value.

The top detractor was Loma Negra (LOMA) (-33.2%, -73 bps), the largest Argentinian cement producer. Net sales decreased 8% year-over-year, primarily driven by a 9.9% decline in the cement segment despite 11.1% volume growth. The low inflation rate made it difficult to pass on prices, while the early stages of the sector recovery faced the low utilisation rates (a feature since c. 2017) of the three main market players, which intensified customer push back on pricing (higher inflation is easier to pass on). EBITDA fell 30.6% in pesos and 32.6% in USD terms. Management anticipates continued volume recovery and price normalization, reaffirming its double-digit growth outlook for 2025. Historically, competition in Argentina's cement market has been rational, so we do not doubt its ability to achieve equilibrium amid inflationary pressures, low utilization and the absence of a functioning mortgage market. In our view, there is significant upside potential once Argentina’s real estate market begins to resemble a more normalized one and government spending picks up again.

The second largest detractor was RHI Magnesita (-29.9% -39 bps), the Austrian-Brazilian refractories company, introduced in our second quarter 2019 letter. The company reported an adjusted EBITA decrease of 26% driven by significant fall in industrial project demand as projects, particularly in glass, were delayed. Steel demand remained low but stable with growth in India and North America countering the structural decline in Europe. RHI experienced a highly competitive pricing environment with continued pressure from Chinese exporters and local players fighting for share in India, East Asia and META in lower value commoditised segments. As a result, the gross profit margin reduced to 20.8% from 24.1% in first half 2024. Management has outlined a plan to boost profitability in the second half of 2025. Key initiatives include fulfilling delayed orders, implementing price increases, reducing SG&A costs, closing a plant in Germany and capturing synergies from the recent Resco acquisition. In our view, RHI is navigating an extremely challenging market environment driven by cyclically lower industrial projects, intense Chinese exports given their weak domestic market and uncertainty caused by tariff negotiations. We think that the new European Commission trade protection measures to support its strategic steel business as well as Brazilian tariffs will aid in the recovery. We see RHI as a global leader in refractory products which is highly correlated to the steel industry, which is at a cyclical low. In this challenging environment, RHI continues to generate cash, and we’re focusing on the company’s through-the-cycle cash generation. If we are right the upside is significant from this point forward.

The third significant detractor was The Italian Sea Group (-11.2%, -38 bps), the Italian luxury yacht builder. The increased uncertainty and the ongoing normalisation phase after the strong growth of recent (post COVID) years, has slowed down order intake. The Italian Sea Group’s order book is comprised of a handful of big pricy orders thus intake is by nature lumpy. The company's average client is ultra-high-net-worth, and the story always was that this group is generally resilient to market cycles. While that could be true, we have a short trading history to help us understand the situation and whether Italian Sea Group is different than the rest of the market known for cyclicality and high bankruptcy rates. In the end, it is a highly discretionary item which could be delayed. That said, there has not been a significant order in well over a year, putting 2025 guidance and 2026 utilization into question. We continue to believe that the Italian Sea Group’s well recognised brands will attract orders eventually, but in the meantime, we are more sceptical about company’s operational leverage and margin resiliency. We prefer to observe from the sidelines for now.

The fourth detractor was Compagnie Del’Odet (-8.6%, -35 bps), the French holding company of Bolloré SE, introduced in our fourth quarter 2024 letter. Bolloré’s most important asset in terms of NAV contribution (c. 47% of total NAV at quarter-end) is Universal Music Group, whose share price declined approximately 14% in the third quarter. Investors seem to be concerned about potential margin pressure from artists holding on to intellectual property rights, giving lower-margin distribution rights to music majors and the ultimate profitability level of the streaming business. Although the publishing business (music rights) is expanding rapidly we have not yet seen much margin pressure. The UMG share price also faced the overhang from the potential full or partial exit of major shareholders such as Pershing Square, the Tencent consortium and Bolloré. Earlier in the year Pershing reduced their stake to reportedly facilitate UMG’s transition to a US listing and position sizing of the fund. Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) Music Entertainment, a Tencent subsidiary, acquired an additional 2% direct stake to the existing 10% stake after the consortium completed a transfer of the shares to its members. Additionally, Cyrille Bolloré resigned from the board in July 2025, signalling a potential change in the nature of the investment and opening a range of possibilities6 such as shrinking the stake or using UMG shares to take out Vivendi due to a potential6 takeout offer, imposed by the AMF. On the Odet front, the company exited its UMG stake (6 million share or 0.33%) at €27.5 per share while directly acquiring a 7.4% stake in Havas and a 2.6% stake in Canal+. Overall, adjusted for the share count Odet trades at 60-70% discount to the value of its underlying assets. Another way to look at it is that Odet trades close to its cash balance, so investors are getting the UMG stake for free.

The fifth largest detractor was Ginebra San Miguel (-5.8%, -30 bps), the Filipino gin and spirits company, which we introduced in our second quarter 2021 letter. Ginebra reported year-on-year growth of 0.5% and 6.8% in volumes and pricing, respectively. The management commented on a bit softer consumer environment and some alcohol volume impact from increased online gaming activity. Amidst all that, Ginebra reported the highest EBIT margin since 2004! Strong cash flow continued, and the net cash balance rose despite the dividend payments. We have no reason to be concerned, and we see further upside. More than half the 30 bps detraction was a result of currency translation, rather than the underlying performance of the business.

Youngone (111770 - KRX)7

Youngone Corporation is a South Korean-based apparel OEM business with an outstanding reputation and longstanding relationships with leading global brands. Youngone also owns 97% of SCOTT Sports, the Swiss-based designer and marketer of premium bicycles. Whilst one may think that a textile manufacturer is a bad business, having commoditized over a century ago, we believe that Youngone serves a market niche which commands premium margins and high returns on capital. However, this has been recently hidden by an underperforming bicycle business, which boomed during COVID and then fizzled, leaving the company with too much inventory. The normalization of the global bicycle market in tandem with management efforts to turnaround the business offers a positive catalyst to the equity story. In the meantime, the combined group trades at an attractive double-digit free cash flow yield with 27% of market cap covered by its net cash position while offering improved shareholder returns with their announced value-up program.

OEM (65-70% Sales)

Youngone Corporation is a top-tier global manufacturer of outdoor and activewear apparel (85% sales), footwear (2%), and gear, including jackets, fleeces, insulated clothing, and technical garments designed for extreme weather conditions. The8 company operates primarily as an OEM/ODM supplier with long-term partnerships with premium global brands like Arc’teryx, The North Face (VFC), lululemon (LULU) and Patagonia. They operate state-of-the-art production facilities in Bangladesh (50% of production), Vietnam (30% of production), China, South Korea and El Salvador. Youngone has the scale to handle high-volume orders while maintaining flexibility to accommodate smaller, specialized runs. They have the expertise to improve fabric’s waterproofing, breathability and warmth beyond the inherent functions of the fabric. OEM manufacturing requires highly efficient production to exacting customer specifications. The company collaborates closely with clients during the design, prototyping and development phases. They must meet stringent working requirements and buyers’ deadlines, in a cost-effective way. As they work closely together and are embedded in their supply chain, customers face increased switching costs. Indeed,7 Original Equipment Manufacturer8 Original Design Manufacturer Youngone’s management confirmed that the North Face and Patagonia relationships are more than 20 years old. Buyers often book several seasons in advance to secure production capacity and the best trained staff. One expert called the business “a near monopoly” in high-end mass-produced technical garments. The success of the business is linked to the performance of its key customers and company’s ability to serve them flawlessly. The top three customers represent 47-50% of the total OEM sales business. Based on customers’ mid-term plans, both Arc’teryx and the North Face expect significant growth ahead while lululemon expects growth in existing and new geographies despite facing short-term challenges.

SCOTT (25-30% Sales)

SCOTT Sports SA, founded in 1958, is a Swiss-based global leader in cycling, winter sports, running and motorsports gear with 89% sales in Europe, 5% in the US and 5% in APAC. SCOTT mainly designs and markets bicycles while using Shimano and SRAM on Giant manufactured frames. SCOTT is a mid-to-premium tier player in the global sports bicycle market, recognized for lightweight, high-performance road, mountain, and e-bikes and is considered a top 10 player in the European market of 11.7 million bikes9 valued at €19.3 billion in 2023. SCOTT competes against Trek, Specialized, Giant and Cannondale. The SCOTT business experienced a classic boom-and-bust cycle during COVID. Pandemic-era demand drove a surge in bike production, creating an oversupply that overwhelmed distribution channels and retailers. Four years on, the supply–demand imbalance remains unresolved. Excess inventory locked up significant capital across the supply chain, from manufacturers to distributors and retailers, while rapid depreciation — given the industry’s short product cycles — intensified competition and forced deep discounting to clear stock. Compounding the challenge, new tariffs on exports to the US have redirected manufacturers’ focus to Europe, where SCOTT has the bulk of their sales, further flooding the market with surplus inventory. SCOTT inventory was roughly 17% of sales in 2021 and ballooned to nearly 70% in the second quarter 2024. The Company is working at reducing the inventory (reported 59% of sales in second quarter 2025) which put pressure on profitability. SCOTT will take more time to recover and will most likely require a general market improvement. In the meantime, after a lengthy dispute with Scott’s founder, Youngone got full control of the9 CONEBI business which could optimize decision-making and capital allocation and allow for a faster turnaround.

Concerns

Both segments are tied to consumer health, which experienced a sharp boom during COVID. The OEM business has remained resilient, supported by the strength and continued growth of its key customers, whose medium-term plans point to further expansion. By contrast, the bicycle segment suffered a post-pandemic demand normalization, with volumes collapsing after the surge. While recovery will take time, the medium-term outlook remains constructive, underpinned by favorable government policies and the growing emphasis on urban mobility. US Tariffs represent an additional concern. The OEM business relies heavily on Bangladeshi operations, which account for roughly 50% of Youngone’s output and currently face a 20% tariff (down from an initial 35%). Likewise, Vietnam, Youngone’s second largest production site has 20% tariffs. To mitigate this impact, Youngone can scale up production at its El Salvador facility (10% tariffs).. Tariff pressures are expected to affect most OEM businesses and are not unique to Youngone and no major textile producing country appears to have a big advantage. The Swiss bicycle business is subject to a steep 39% tariff, though the US market represents only a small portion of SCOTT’s sales (5% direct sales). We view these tariffs largely as a negotiation tactic, but they do reduce short-term visibility. However, bike manufacturers have sent excess inventory to Europe to avoid US tariffs, putting further pressure on the market.

Valuation

Youngone shares trade at < 6.6x earnings excluding net cash (27% of the market capitalization). Despite the low multiple and the cash rich balance sheet the company offers only a 2.4% dividend yield. To enhance shareholder value, Youngone announced a Value-Up plan to improve shareholder returns. This includes maintaining return on equity above 10%, improving the price to book ratio to at least 0.8x by 2027 and 1x by 2030 (approaching the 2020 level of 0.8x and 2021 level of 0.9x) and increasing the dividend payout ratio to 25% by 2027 (from roughly 14% over the last five years). The company has bought back roughly 2% over the last five quarters.
